title: Submit a report
definition: To send or hand in a completed report to your manager
example1: Please submit the sales report by Friday.
example2: I submitted my report yesterday, but my boss hasn't reviewed it yet.
usage1: Use "submit to" + person: Submit to your supervisor. \br Use "submit by" + deadline: Submit by 5 PM.

title: Deadline
definition: The date or time by which a task must be finished
example1: The deadline for this project is next Monday.
example2: I missed the deadline and had to ask for an extension.
usage1: "Meet a deadline" = finish on time. \br "Miss a deadline" = finish late.

title: Cover letter
definition: A letter sent with your resume to explain why you want a job
example1: I attached my cover letter and resume to the email.
example2: Your cover letter should be one page maximum.
usage1: Customize each cover letter for the specific job.

title: Follow up
definition: To contact someone again after a previous meeting or message
example1: I'll follow up with the client next week.
example2: Thanks for the interview. I'm just following up to see if you've made a decision.
usage1: Send a follow-up email 3-5 days after an interview.

title: Onboard
definition: To integrate a new employee into a company
example1: HR will onboard the new hires on Monday.
example2: The onboarding process includes filling out tax forms and setting up your email.
usage1: "New hire" = a person who just started.

title: Remote work
definition: Working from home or outside the office
example1: I've been doing remote work for two years.
example2: Remote work saves me two hours of commute every day.
usage1: Synonym: telecommuting

title: Meeting agenda
definition: A list of topics to discuss in a meeting
example1: Can you send the meeting agenda before we start?
example2: The agenda has five items, so we should finish in an hour.
usage1: Stick to the agenda to keep meetings short.

title: Action item
definition: A task assigned to someone during a meeting
example1: Your action item is to contact the IT department.
example2: I completed all my action items from yesterday's meeting.
usage1: Write down action items so you don't forget.

title: Stakeholder
definition: A person or group with interest in a project's outcome
example1: We need approval from all stakeholders before launching.
example2: The main stakeholders are investors, customers, and employees.
usage1: Keep stakeholders updated regularly.

title: Office - Late to Meeting
definition: You are 5 minutes late to a meeting. What do you say?
sentence: Sorry I'm late. Traffic was bad.
correct_answer: Apologize briefly and give a short reason. Don't make excuses or blame others.
explanation: Sorry I'm late. Let me catch up. What did I miss?

title: Office - Asking for Vacation
definition: How do you request time off from your manager?
sentence: Could I request a few days off next month?
correct_answer: Politely request using "Could I" or "May I". Give advance notice.
explanation: Could I take three days off starting the 15th? I have available vacation days.

title: Office - Computer Problem
definition: Your computer won't turn on. Who do you call?
sentence: IT support.
correct_answer: IT support handles technology problems. Most companies have a help desk number or ticket system.
explanation: My computer is frozen. Can you send someone from IT to my desk?

title: Office - Answering Phone
definition: You answer a call at work. What do you say?
sentence: Good morning, ABC Company. This is Maria speaking.
correct_answer: State the company name and your name. It sounds professional and helps the caller know they dialed correctly.
explanation: Good afternoon, Smith Industries. How may I direct your call?

title: Office - Asking for Help
definition: You don't know how to use the new software. What do you say to a coworker?
sentence: Could you show me how this works when you have a minute?
correct_answer: Politely ask for help and offer to work around their schedule. Don't demand.
explanation: Could you walk me through this report? I want to learn it myself for next time.

title: Office - Email Sign-off
sentence: please let me know if you have any ______
correct_answer: questions|concerns
explanation: Common professional sign-offs: "Please let me know if you have any questions."
example1: Best regards, Thomas \br Please let me know if you need anything else.

title: Office - CC in Email
sentence: please ______ me on that email
correct_answer: cc
explanation: "CC" means carbon copy. It sends a copy to someone who needs to see it but isn't the main recipient.
example1: Please CC my manager so she knows the status.

title: Office - Voicemail
sentence: please leave a ______ after the beep
correct_answer: message
explanation: The standard voicemail greeting: "You've reached [name]. I'm unavailable. Please leave a message after the beep."
example1: You've reached John. I'm away from my desk. Please leave your name and number.

title: Polite Office Requests
definition: Structure for asking colleagues politely \br regular \br Would you mind + verb-ing + ?
question_text: Would you mind closing the door? \br Would you mind sending me that file? \br Could you please + verb?
sentence: Would you mind speaking more quietly? I'm on a call.
opt1: Could you please forward me the meeting notes?
optopt3: "Would you mind" is followed by verb-ing. Answer "Not at all" if you agree.

title: Setting Deadlines
definition: Structure for giving due dates \br regular \br Please have + [task] + done by + [date/time]
question_text: Please have the report done by Friday. \br Please finish this by 3 PM today.
sentence: Please have the invoices ready by end of day.
opt1: I need the draft by Tuesday morning.
optopt3: Be specific with times and time zones for remote teams.

title: How to write a professional email
definition: Subject line, greeting, body, closing, signature
question_text: Subject: Q3 Report Draft \br \br Dear Ms. Chen, \br \br Please find attached the draft. \br \br Best regards, \br \br Alex
sentence: Keep subject lines short and clear. \br Use "Dear [Name]" for formal, "Hi [Name]" for casual.
opt1: Body: state purpose in first sentence. \br Closing: Best regards, Sincerely, Thanks.
opt2: Always proofread before sending. Never write in all caps.

title: Shift
definition: A scheduled period of work
example1: My shift starts at 6 AM
example2: so I wake up very early.
example3: I'm working the night shift this week.
tip: Day shift = morning/afternoon. Night shift = overnight. Split shift = two separate periods.

title: Overtime
definition: Time worked beyond regular hours
example1: We had to work overtime to meet the deadline.
example2: Overtime is paid at 1.5 times my normal rate.
usage1: "Time and a half" = 1.5x pay. "Double time" = 2x pay.

title: Break room
definition: A room where employees can rest
sentence: or have coffee
example3: I'm heating my lunch in the break room microwave.
usage1: The break room has a fridge, coffee machine, and vending machines.
column20: Some companies have a "break room" and a separate "lunch room".

title: Time clock
definition: A machine that records when employees start and stop work
example1: Don't forget to punch in when you arrive.
example2: I forgot to punch out yesterday, so HR had to fix my timesheet.
usage1: "Punch in" = start work. "Punch out" = end work.

title: Uniform
definition: A special set of clothes worn for work
example1: All factory workers must wear a uniform and safety vest.
example2: My uniform has my name embroidered on the chest.
usage1: Some jobs provide uniforms for free. Others make you buy them.

title: Factory - Safety First
definition: You see a coworker not wearing safety goggles. What do you say?
sentence: Hey, don't forget your safety goggles. It's the rule.
opt1: I don't care what you do.
correct_answer: Politely remind them of safety rules. It protects everyone, including you.
explanation: The sign says safety glasses required in this area. Let me grab you a pair.

title: Factory - Machine Problem
definition: A machine starts making a strange noise. What do you do?
sentence: Stop the machine and tell your supervisor.
correct_answer: Safety first. Stop the machine and report it. Never fix machinery unless you're trained.
explanation: The press is making a grinding sound. I shut it down and need maintenance.

title: Factory - Heavy Lifting
definition: A box is too heavy for you to lift alone. What do you say?
sentence: Could you help me lift this? It's too heavy alone.
correct_answer: Ask for help directly. Lifting alone can cause injury. Team lifting is safer.
explanation: This box is over 50 pounds. Can you grab the other side so we can lift together?

title: Factory - Safety Gear
sentence: you must wear ______ protection in this area
correct_answer: hearing|ear
explanation: "Hearing protection" = earplugs or earmuffs. Factories with loud machines require this.
example1: Hearing protection is mandatory beyond this yellow line.

title: Factory - Inventory
sentence: we need to ______ the parts before the next shift
correct_answer: count
explanation: "Count inventory" = count how many items you have. Done at shift start or end.
example1: Can you count the bolts? We need 500 for tomorrow's order.

title: Factory Instructions (Imperatives)
definition: Giving clear commands \br regular \br Verb + object + location/time
question_text: Put the boxes on the pallet. \br Stack them no higher than five. \br Label each box before moving it.
sentence: Wear your gloves at all times. \br Report any accidents immediately.
opt1: Use the forklift for heavy loads.
opt2: "Imperative" means command form. No subject needed.

title: Forklift
definition: A vehicle used to lift and move heavy pallets in a factory/warehouse
example1: Only certified workers can operate the forklift.
example2: The forklift can carry up to 3,000 pounds.
usage1: Forklift certification requires training and a test.

title: Pick and pack
definition: To select items from shelves and put them in boxes for shipping
example1: The warehouse team picks and packs about 500 orders per day.
example2: I work in pick and pack. We start at 4 AM.
usage1: Accuracy is more important than speed.

title: Quality control
definition: Checking products to make sure they meet standards
example1: The item failed quality control because of a scratch.
example2: Quality control rejected 10% of this batch.
usage1: QC is often a separate department.

title: Assembly line
definition: A sequence of workers and machines where each person adds a part
example1: Working on the assembly line is repetitive but fast-paced.
example2: The assembly line moves at one car every 90 seconds.
usage1: Each worker has one specific task.

title: Lean manufacturing
definition: A production method focused on reducing waste and increasing efficiency
question_text: The five principles: value, value stream, flow, pull, perfection. \br \br Goal = do more with less (time, inventory, space).
example1: Toyota invented lean manufacturing. \br "Just-in-time" means parts arrive exactly when needed.
example2: Reduce waste = excess motion, waiting, defects, overproduction.

title: Attendance
definition: Being present at work or school
example1: Your attendance record shows three absences this month.
example2: Perfect attendance = never missed a day.
usage1: Tardy = late. Absent = not there.

title: School - Late to Class
definition: You arrive 10 minutes late to class. What do you do?
sentence: Quietly enter and sit down. Apologize to the teacher after class.
opt1: Walk in loudly and announce you're here.
correct_answer: Enter quietly to avoid disturbing other students. Apologize after class, not during the lesson.
explanation: Sorry I'm late. The bus was delayed. May I come in?

title: School - Forgot Homework
definition: You forgot your homework. What do you say to the teacher?
sentence: I'm sorry. I forgot my homework. Can I bring it tomorrow?
opt1: I did it but I lost it.
correct_answer: Be honest and take responsibility. Offer a solution (bring it tomorrow). Lying makes it worse.
explanation: My homework is at home. May I email it to you tonight?

title: School - Group Project
definition: A group member isn't doing their share of the work. What do you say to them?
sentence: Can we divide the tasks more clearly? I want to make sure everyone knows their part.
correct_answer: Address the problem directly but politely. Focus on solutions, not blame.
explanation: Which part can you take? We need to finish the slides by Thursday.

title: School - Asking for Help
definition: You don't understand the math lesson. What do you say to the teacher?
sentence: Could you explain that again? I didn't fully understand.
correct_answer: Politely ask for clarification. Most teachers are happy to help students who ask.
explanation: Could you go over the last part one more time? I want to make sure I get it.

title: School - Raising Hand
sentence: you need to ______ your hand before speaking
correct_answer: raise
explanation: In most classrooms, you raise your hand to get the teacher's attention. Don't shout out.
example1: Please raise your hand if you know the answer.

title: School - Final Exam
sentence: the final exam counts for 50 ______ of our grade
correct_answer: percent
explanation: "Percent" = per hundred. If the exam is 50%, it's half your total grade.
example1: The final is 40% of your grade, so study hard.

title: School - Hall Pass
sentence: you need a ______ ______ to leave the classroom
correct_answer: hall pass
explanation: A hall pass is a note or lanyard that shows permission to be in the hallway during class.
example1: Can I have a hall pass? I need to go to the nurse.

title: Classroom Requests
definition: Polite phrases for students \br regular \br May I + [verb] + please?
question_text: May I go to the bathroom? \br May I borrow a pencil? \br May I sharpen my pencil?
sentence: May I turn in my homework late? \br May I sit here? \br May I ask a question?
opt2: Use "May I" for polite requests. "Can I" is acceptable but less formal.

title: School Subjects (Present Simple)
definition: Talking about your classes \br regular \br Subject + has/have + [class] + on + [day]
question_text: I have math on Mondays and Wednesdays. \br She has history on Tuesdays. \br We have PE on Friday afternoons.
sentence: What classes do you have tomorrow? \br I have chemistry first period. \br My favorite subject is biology.
opt2: Use "period" for class blocks: first period = 8-9 AM, second period = 9-10 AM.

title: How to study for a test
definition: Active recall, spaced repetition, practice questions
question_text: Active recall = close the book and say the answer. \br Spaced repetition = review after 1 day, 3 days, 1 week, 1 month.
sentence: Don't just re-read. Re-reading feels productive but isn't. Do practice problems.
opt1: Teach someone else. If you can explain it, you know it.

title: Enroll
definition: To officially join a class or school
example1: I want to enroll in the advanced English course.
example2: Enrollment ends on Friday. Don't wait until the last day.
usage1: You enroll in a course/school. You register for a class.

title: Syllabus
definition: A document that outlines the course topics
sentence: and grading
example3: Check the syllabus for the due date.
usage1: The syllabus says no late work accepted.

title: Plagiarism
definition: Using someone else's work without giving credit
example1: Plagiarism can get you expelled from school.
example2: Always cite your sources to avoid plagiarism.
usage1: Paraphrasing still requires citation if the idea isn't yours.

title: Scholarship
definition: Free money for school based on merit or need
example1: I applied for a scholarship to help pay tuition.
example2: She won a full scholarship for all four years.
usage1: Grants = need-based. Scholarships = merit-based usually.

title: School - Parent-Teacher Conference
definition: What happens at a parent-teacher conference?
sentence: Parents meet with the teacher to discuss the student's progress.
correct_answer: The teacher shows grades, behavior, and areas for improvement. Parents ask questions.
explanation: My mom learned that I'm doing well in science but need to participate more in English.

title: School - Transcript
definition: What is a transcript?
sentence: An official record of all classes taken and grades received.
correct_answer: Colleges and employers request transcripts to verify your academic record.
explanation: I need to send my transcript to three universities.

title: Internship
definition: A temporary job to gain experience
example2: I did a summer internship at a marketing agency.
example3: The internship was unpaid, but I learned a lot.
tip: Internships can lead to full-time job offers.

title: Major
definition: The main subject a student studies in college
example1: I declared my major as computer science.
example2: What's your major? I'm an English major.
usage1: Minor = secondary subject.

title: Campus
definition: The buildings and grounds of a college or university
example1: The campus has a library
example2: gym
usage1: I live off campus, about a 15-minute walk away.

title: School - Drop a Class
sentence: i had to ______ the class because it was too difficult
correct_answer: drop
explanation: "Drop a class" = remove it from your schedule before the deadline. Different from "withdraw" (later deadline).
example1: I'm going to drop chemistry and take biology instead.

title: School - Prerequisite
sentence: you have to take math 101 as a ______ for math 201
correct_answer: prerequisite
explanation: A prerequisite is a class you must complete before taking a more advanced class.
example1: Calculus has a prerequisite of pre-calculus or algebra.

title: College Application Process
definition: Steps to apply to university \br regular \br first + then + finally
question_text: First take the entrance exam, then write your essay. \br Submit your transcript and letters of recommendation.
sentence: First research schools, then narrow your list to 5-8 colleges. \br Request your counselor to send transcripts.
opt2: Early decision = binding. Early action = non-binding. Regular decision = standard deadline.

title: Financial aid explained
definition: Money to help pay for college including grants
sentence: and work-study
opt1: Grants = free money (Pell Grant). Loans = borrowed money you pay back. Work-study = part-time job on campus.
example3: FAFSA = Free Application for Federal Student Aid. Fill it out every year.
usage1: Scholarships are also free money but based on merit (grades, sports, etc.).
tip: Don't borrow more than your expected first-year salary.

title: Patient
definition: A person receiving medical treatment
example1: The patient is waiting to see the doctor.
example2: The patient complained of chest pain.
usage1: "Outpatient" = not staying overnight. "Inpatient" = staying in hospital.

title: Appointment
definition: A scheduled time to see a doctor or specialist
example1: I need to make an appointment with my dentist.
example2: The next available appointment is next Tuesday.
usage1: Cancel 24 hours in advance or pay a fee.

title: Refill
definition: A replacement supply of medicine
example1: I need a refill on my blood pressure medication.
example2: You can request a refill online or by phone.
usage1: Most prescriptions have 3-6 refills before needing a doctor's visit.

title: Hospital - Emergency
definition: When should you go to the emergency room (ER)?
sentence: For life-threatening conditions like chest pain, severe bleeding, or trouble breathing.
correct_answer: The ER is for emergencies. For non-emergencies, see your regular doctor or urgent care.
explanation: Call 911 or go directly to the ER if you think someone is having a stroke or heart attack.

title: Hospital - Symptoms Description
definition: You have a fever and cough. How do you tell the doctor?
sentence: I've had a fever and cough for three days.
correct_answer: Be specific: what symptom, how long, how severe. This helps the doctor diagnose you.
explanation: My fever is 101 degrees and the cough is keeping me awake at night.

title: Hospital - Triage
sentence: the nurse will ______ patients based on how urgent their condition is
correct_answer: triage
explanation: Triage = sorting patients by urgency. Used in emergencies and wars. Life-threatening cases go first.
example1: In the ER, a heart attack patient gets triaged ahead of someone with a sprained ankle.

title: Doctor's Questions (Present Perfect)
definition: Asking about duration of symptoms \br regular \br How long + have/has + been + verb-ing?
question_text: How long have you been feeling this way? \br How long have you had the cough? \br Have you taken any medicine for it?
sentence: How long have you been having these headaches? \br Have you had a fever? \br When did the pain start?
opt2: Use present perfect for symptoms that started in the past and continue now.

title: Order
definition: A request for food or products (restaurant or retail)
example1: I placed an order for a coffee and sandwich.
example2: Your order will be ready in 10 minutes.
usage1: "On order" = you ordered it but haven't received it yet.

title: Refund
definition: Getting your money back for a returned product
example1: I returned the shoes and got a full refund.
example2: We offer refunds within 30 days with a receipt.
usage1: Store credit = money you can only spend in that store.

title: Exchange
definition: Swapping a product for a different size or color
example1: Can I exchange this for a medium? It's too small.
example2: Exchanges are free, but you pay the difference if the new item costs more.
usage1: You need the original receipt for most exchanges.

title: Retail - Customer Complaint
definition: A customer says the product is broken. What do you say as a cashier?
sentence: I'm sorry about that. Let me get you a replacement or a refund.
correct_answer: Apologize first, then offer a solution (refund, exchange, replacement). Stay calm even if the customer is angry.
explanation: I'm very sorry that happened. Do you have your receipt? I can process a return right now.

title: Retail - Out of Stock
definition: A customer asks for an item you don't have. What do you say?
sentence: I'm sorry, we're out of stock. I can check if another store has it.
correct_answer: Apologize and offer an alternative (other store, online, back in stock date).
explanation: Let me check our system to see when we'll get more. Would you like me to hold one for you?

title: Retail - Receipt
sentence: can i have a ______ please
correct_answer: receipt
explanation: A receipt shows proof of purchase. You need it for returns, exchanges, or expense reports.
example1: Here's your receipt. Your warranty is valid for one year.

title: Retail - Clearance
sentence: these items are on ______ for 50% off
correct_answer: clearance
explanation: "Clearance" = final sale, no returns. Used for seasonal items or products being discontinued.
example1: The clearance section is in the back of the store. Everything is 40-70% off.

title: Retail - Helping a Customer
definition: Polite retail phrases \br regular \br Are you looking for + something specific?
question_text: Are you looking for anything specific today? \br Can I help you find something? \br Let me know if you have any questions.
sentence: Are you being helped? \br That's over in aisle 7. \br Would you like me to hold that at the register for you?
opt2: Make eye contact and smile. Acknowledge every customer who enters.

title: Blueprint
definition: A detailed technical drawing showing how to build something
example1: The construction workers follow the blueprint every day.
example2: The blueprint shows electrical wiring and plumbing.
usage1: Make copies of blueprints so originals stay clean.

title: Hard hat
definition: A protective helmet worn on construction sites
example1: Hard hat required beyond this point.
example2: My hard hat has my name written inside.
usage1: Hard hats expire. Check the date inside.

title: Harness
definition: Safety straps that prevent a worker from falling
example1: You need to wear a harness when working on the scaffold.
example2: Clip your harness to the anchor point before stepping onto the beam.
usage1: Inspect harness for cuts or damage before each use.

title: Construction - Safety Violation
definition: You see a worker not wearing a hard hat. What do you do?
sentence: Politely remind them to put on their hard hat.
correct_answer: A polite reminder is usually enough. Some workers forget. Escalate only if they refuse repeatedly.
explanation: Hey, don't forget your hard hat. The foreman is doing a safety walk in 10 minutes.

title: Construction - Tool Name
sentence: hand me the ______ to hit this nail
correct_answer: hammer
explanation: A hammer hits nails. A mallet hits wood (non-marring). A sledgehammer is very heavy.
example1: Can you pass me the hammer? This nail is stubborn.

title: Construction - Measurement
sentence: i need to ______ the wood before cutting it
correct_answer: measure
explanation: Measure twice, cut once. This is a famous rule in construction.
example1: Let me measure this again. I think my first measurement was wrong.

title: Construction Commands (Imperatives)
definition: Direct instructions for safety \br regular \br [verb] + [object] + [location]
question_text: Wear your hard hat at all times. \br Tie off your harness before climbing. \br Keep the area clean.
sentence: Check your tools before starting. \br Report any damaged equipment. \br Never walk under a raised load.
opt2: Safety violations can get you removed from the site.

title: Time sheet
definition: A form to record hours worked
example1: Fill out your time sheet before Friday.
example2: My time sheet shows 40 regular hours and 5 overtime hours.
usage1: Submit time sheets on time to get paid.

title: PTO (Paid Time Off)
definition: Vacation days that you get paid for
example1: I have two weeks of PTO saved up.
example2: I need to request PTO at least two weeks in advance.
usage1: Use PTO before it expires. Some companies have "use it or lose it" policies.

title: Direct deposit
definition: Payment sent automatically to your bank account
example1: I signed up for direct deposit so I don't have to cash checks.
example2: My paycheck arrives by direct deposit every other Friday.
usage1: You need your bank routing and account numbers to set this up.

title: ID badge
definition: A card with your photo and name for building access
example1: Scan your ID badge to enter the building.
example2: I lost my ID badge and need a replacement. It costs $10.
usage1: Wear your badge above the waist where security can see it.

title: Non-disclosure agreement (NDA)
definition: A legal contract to keep company information secret
example1: Everyone signed an NDA before starting the project.
example2: The NDA says I can't share customer data with anyone outside the company.
usage1: Breaking an NDA can get you sued or fired.

title: Workplace - Confidential Information
definition: A friend asks about a secret project at work. What do you say?
sentence: I'm sorry, I can't discuss that. It's confidential.
opt1: I'll tell you but don't tell anyone.
correct_answer: Protect confidential information. Saying "I can't discuss it" is professional and safe.
explanation: I signed an NDA. I'm not allowed to share any details about the new product until launch.

title: Workplace - Performance Review
definition: What happens at a performance review?
sentence: Your manager discusses your strengths, weaknesses, and goals for the next period.
correct_answer: Reviews happen annually or semi-annually. Prepare by listing your accomplishments.
explanation: My review went well. My manager said I exceeded expectations in customer service.

title: Workplace - Resignation
sentence: i am writing to ______ my resignation
correct_answer: submit
explanation: "Submit my resignation" = formally quit your job. Usually you give two weeks' notice.
example1: Please accept this letter as my formal resignation, effective two weeks from today.

title: Asking for Clarification in Meetings
definition: Polite phrases when you don't understand \br regular \br Could you + please + rephrase/elaborate?
question_text: Could you please rephrase that? I didn't follow. \br Could you elaborate on the second point?
sentence: Just to clarify, are you saying we need to finish by Friday? \br Let me make sure I understand. You want the report by Tuesday?
opt2: Never pretend you understand. Ask immediately, not after the meeting.

title: How to ask for a raise
definition: Prepare, time it right, use data, practice
question_text: Step 1: Research market salary for your role. Step 2: List your accomplishments (specific numbers). Step 3: Schedule a private meeting with your manager.
sentence: "Based on my research and my performance, I'd like to discuss a salary adjustment to $X."
opt1: Timing: during performance review or after a big win. Not during a crisis or right after layoffs.
opt2: Practice out loud first. Be confident but not aggressive. Be ready for "no" and ask what you need to do to get to yes.
